Okay. So this is a little bit like, when we're looking for work, we utilize our network to our very best advantage. So the very best way to do that is make it as clear and concise as possible, what we're looking for.

So in your case, if there's particular skills that they must have, we need to make it really simple for them. Almost like an elevator pitch. How could you term it in a way that somebody could remember that and then pass that on to somebody they might meet? The short.
It's not like the job ad necessarily, but it's short and sharp and we use everybody we have in our network. I mean, I want anybody you've ever met to know that you are looking for somebody. Yeah.

So the onboarding part of this process and the consistent feedback and management of them will be even more important than it normally is because we haven't set the tone with the experience when we're going through the group interviews, the questions, the screening, all of that kind of stuff isn't already setting the tone.

So we would need to manage them quite closely. But I think the broader and the wider you get it out there, the more likely you are to pick up somebody out of the network that you have. And your network includes anybody you have ever spoken to and you have an email address for.

Again, if you understand what you're gunning towards, what it is that you want the outcome to be, that will help you with the clarity around making that decision.

So if the outcome is for good clear communication with your team, then depending on your team, that may look like that daily huddle where we have a super skilled team that can understand everything and we've got the ability as business owners to communicate clearly what our expectations are for an entire week.
You may only need to check in once or twice a week on a Monday and a Friday, for example.

But if we've got people that we need to lead a little closer, then we'd probably have a daily check in to see how they're tracking. You would still. Even though I know these guys are likely to be subbies for you, I would still be doing toolbox talks on a weekly basis.
That's just going to cover any of those frustrations that come up for you and you can still talk to them about their performance.

It's a bit harder to like use a scorecard or to have quarterly reviews in one form or another or even one on ones, but you can still check in and be giving regular feedback about their performance, which I know will be challenging for Brad because he's such a nice person.

He's not going to want to push on someone when they're not delivering. But if we've communicated clearly what the expectations are, kind of makes it a lot easier for you to push back when the expectations aren't being met.
And if we do that on a little bite size basis, like on a daily basis, A, as a business owner, we're developing that muscle, it gets much easier to flex.
